issue_title: Version on PyPi out of date, raises AttributeError on parse_headers
issue_body:
As of 2016-10-29, the rfc6266 package on PyPi (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/rfc6266) is dated 2013-11-13.  There is a typo in that version that has been fixed in the version on Github.  Line 28 reads:

`LOGGER.addHandler(logging.NullHandler)`

instead of:

`LOGGER.addHandler(logging.NullHandler())`

So the call to LOGGER.debug inside parse_headers raises an AttributeError.

issue_title: PubSub get_message() not always returns None when there're no data available
issue_body:
Hello Jason,

Looks like PubSub get_message() function is not working as expected, on  just created channel without no data. I assume that call of this method should return None in that case, however None returns only once and then execution "hangs" and awaits for coroutine to be completed (actual data appears in the channel) and then execution continues.

Here are the steps to reproduce this issue:

```
# 1. Create some subscription (PubSub obj)

# redis — initialized connection to some Redis instance
pbsb = redis.pubsub(ignore_subscribe_messages=True)
await pbsb.subscribe("some-channel")

# 2. Query message in the loop
timeout = 5
message = None
end_time = time.time() + timeout
while timeout == 0 or time.time() < end_time:
    print(1)
    message = await pbsb.get_message(timeout=1)
    print(2)

# 3. results
>>> 1
>>> 2
>>> 1
# these should be a little more 1 and 2 :)

# 4. Nothing more happened until some data arrives, then
>>> 2
```

I've attached a [patch](https://github.com/NoneGG/aredis/files/1308292/get_message.patch.txt)
 addressed to resolve this issue, it removes `connection.can_read()` check in **aredis/pubsub.py** on line 132, because in that case it always returns `False` and adds several imports:
```
from aredis.exceptions import ConnectionError
from aredis.exceptions import TimeoutError
```
Looks like without them `except` on line 112 is not working as expected.

Also in **aredis/connection.py** on line 307 I've added check
```
if not self._reader:
    break
```

Because when coroutine actually cancelled, `self._reader` is getting None and this is leads to unwanted exception.

Best,
Jack

issue_title: Add gamma method to Image class
issue_body: Add the ability to perform a gamma analysis on an arbitrary Image. This is probably easiest to implement by adding a method to the [base image class](http://pylinac.readthedocs.org/en/latest/core_modules.html#pylinac.core.image.ImageMixin). The gamma algorithm is already [implemented for the log analyzer](http://pylinac.readthedocs.org/en/latest/log_analyzer.html#pylinac.log_analyzer.GammaFluence.calc_map); it simply needs to be generalized for an Image.

A common way to use Statsd is to create a client for a given process and continually reuse it.  That is fine, except for an annoying implementation detail that I noticed while securing a box.

When data is first sent via `socket` ( https://github.com/jsocol/pystatsd/blob/master/statsd/client.py#L145-L151 ) Python appears to bind on all interfaces (e.g. `0.0.0.0`) to a random port in effort of accepting a response.  (sidenote: it took a while to pinpoint this onto pystatsd).  It would make sense to note this inherent behavior in the docs/readme for the next person who is securing their box.

The Ruby version of VCR supports a [decode_compressed_response](https://www.relishapp.com/vcr/vcr/v/2-9-3/docs/cassettes/decode-compressed-response) option that decompresses response bodies encoded with gzip or deflate before recording and makes cassettes human-readable/editable. This could be implemented in vcrpy as a before_record_response function using zlib.

issue_title: Add ability to specify an alternate inventory file.
issue_body:
Most of the ability to specify an alternate inventory file already exists.  Simply modifying **utilities.py** line 53 to accept a "file_name" as a parameter completes the functionality.

**old line 53:**  
`def load_devices():`

**new line 53:** 
`def load_devices(file_name=None):`

The code to forward the parameter _file_name_ to the _find_cfg_file_ function already exists in the body of the _load_devices_ function.

I've found that adding the parm to **utilities.py** line 53 does not affect backward compatibility (_i.e. running without specifying a file_) and also degrades gracefully if the file is not found - defaulting back to using the standard .netmiko.yml file.  The file can be specified via full path or relative addressing.

Adding this ability allows us to manage a large inventory among numerous team members working on existing and new (not yet deployed) equipment without mangling a large master inventory file.  We can also maintain inventory files by device type, location, etc.

issue_title: directory with tiles as input option
issue_body:
Add an abstract input type where a directory (e.g. a written output from another mapchete process) can be provided.

e.g.:
```yaml
input:
  dir_input:
    format: TileDir
    path: /path/to/dir
    metatiling: 2
    type: geodetic
    bounds: # optional    
```

Should also work for remote "directories" like tiled data on an object store accessible via HTTP.

- `hiku.sources.graph.Expr`: `type` argument should be required
- `hiku.sources.sqlalchemy.FieldsQuery`: `primary_key` argument should be keyword-only

Hi Again,

I've noticed a weird bug with pywal interacting with my Mac's Xquartz (x11) installation.   As far as I can tell, whenever I run the `wal` command, and the xquartz app is not open, the command will freeze when refreshing the environments, and just sit there.

I've tracked down the error specifically to `xrdb` command in `reload.py` (line 19/20ish).   It seems that the xrdb command will freeze on mac and wait for xquartz to open if it isn't open already.

Anyway, I fixed the issue by chaning line 18 of `reload.py`.

I changed
`if shutil.which("xrdb"):` 
to 
`if shutil.which("xrdb") and OS != "Darwin":`)

I also added `OS` to the import from .settings (Line 10), so I could use that variable there.

Once this was added, it bypassed the xrdb command and allowed the env to refresh normally.

(I haven't used python in a few years, so there may be a simpler fix for this, but it worked for me.)

---

I also tested pywal without xquartz installed, and everything worked correctly, so it seems to only happen when Xquartz is installed.

I wish I could help figure out why this issue was happening with Xquartz, but I'm not familiar enough with how X11 works on mac to be able to.

issue_title: Timestamp conversion does not handle daylight time
issue_body:
It looks like `udatetime` does not handle changing utc offset for daylight savings time at different times of year, so the unit tests that use local timezone are failing when run in US Eastern Timezone. 

Here is the problem: https://github.com/freach/udatetime/blob/master/src/rfc3339.c#L74
`udatetime` uses the same UTC offset (in my case -4) for all dates, but `datetime` correctly uses -5 in January and -4 in August

```console
In[2]: import udatetime
In[3]: from datetime import datetime

In[4]: udatetime.fromtimestamp(1471046400)
Out[4]: datetime.datetime(2016, 8, 12, 20, 0, tzinfo=--4:00)
In[5]: datetime.fromtimestamp(1471046400)
Out[5]: datetime.datetime(2016, 8, 12, 20, 0)

In[6]: udatetime.fromtimestamp(1451606400)
Out[6]: datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 31, 20, 0, tzinfo=--4:00)
In[7]: datetime.fromtimestamp(1451606400)
Out[7]: datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 31, 19, 0)

In[8]: udatetime.fromtimestamp(0)
Out[8]: datetime.datetime(1969, 12, 31, 20, 0, tzinfo=--4:00)
In[9]: datetime.fromtimestamp(0)
Out[9]: datetime.datetime(1969, 12, 31, 19, 0)

```
Recommend changing the unit tests to avoid this problem buy using utcfromtimestamp() in the tests that do not supply a timestamp.  

In future, consider avoiding the problem entirely by always assuming that any datetime that does not come with an explicit timezone offset is UTC and never use the local timezone implicitly.   Since this library is all about high performance for high volume throughput, it seems likely that most use cases will be using UTC anyway.

I like this tokenizer better than using `tokenize`

 I thought standard tokenize module would be optimized in C, but it's fully written in Python and since it has so much more than we actually need, I'm sure it's not as efficient. Furthermore, I'd go as far as yielding the token, tokstr and the position, it being either an offset or a (row, col) pair, which for most cases would be enough:

```python
def tokenizer(expr, symbol_class=boolean.Symbol):
    if not isinstance(expr, basestring):
        raise TypeError("expr must be string but it is %s." % type(expr))

    TOKENS = {
        '*': boolean.TOKEN_AND,
        '&': boolean.TOKEN_AND,
        'and': boolean.TOKEN_AND,
        '|': boolean.TOKEN_OR,
        '+': boolean.TOKEN_OR,
        'or': boolean.TOKEN_OR,
        '~': boolean.TOKEN_NOT,
        '!': boolean.TOKEN_NOT,
        'not': boolean.TOKEN_NOT,
        '(': boolean.TOKEN_LPAR,
        '[': boolean.TOKEN_LPAR,
        ']': boolean.TOKEN_RPAR,
        ')': boolean.TOKEN_RPAR,
        'true': boolean.TRUE,
        '1': boolean.TRUE,
        'false': boolean.FALSE,
        '0': boolean.FALSE,
        'none': boolean.FALSE,
    }

    length = len(expr)
    offset = 0
    while offset < length:
        tok = expr[offset]

        sym = tok.isalpha() or tok == '_'
        if sym:
            offset += 1
            while offset < length:
                char = expr[offset]
                if char.isalnum() or char in ('.', ':', '_'):
                    offset += 1
                    tok += char
                else:
                    break
            offset -= 1

        try:
            yield TOKENS[tok.lower()], tok, offset
        except KeyError:
            if sym:
                yield symbol_class(tok), tok, offset
            elif tok not in (' ', '\t', '\r', '\n'):
                raise TypeError("Unknown token %s at position %s." % (tok, offset))

        offset += 1
```

issue_title: Use Gulp or Grunt for frontend dev.
Allow characteristic properties like minValue to be overridden.
issue_body:
It should be possible to configure the properties of a characteristic to align with the functionality of the device and the iOS UI. For example, say your window covering can go up only to 50% open and that it is either closed or open. HAP specifies the default values for the coverings target position as maxValue=100 and minStep=0.1. This means iOS clients can slide at 0.1 step all the way to 100%, which you must map to all-up or all-down in your code.

Instead, you can do:
```python
service = loader.get_serv_loader().get("WindowCovering")
pos = service.get_characteristic("TargetPosition")
pos.properties["maxValue"] = 50
pos.properties["minStep"] = 50
pos.hap_template = pos._create_hap_template()
```

Now iOS correctly allows you to only fully go up or down and the max display value is 50%. The hacky thing about the above is the last line and this is the topic of this issue - overriding properties should be more "native".

issue_title: Add a means to specify region when using profile
issue_body:
This is an enhancement request to add a "--region" command-line option to complement the existing "--profile" one.

Right now, it is quite easy to run ```awslogs groups --profile production``` or ```awslogs groups --profile staging``` with appropriately configured ~/.aws/config and ~/.aws/credentials files (and delegated/federated AWS access credentials).  However, if you have specified the default "region" alongside the profile stored in your ~/.aws/config file, there is no easy way to override the region when using the awslogs command line.

For example, your ~/.aws/config might contain:
```
# ...
[profile something]
# not really needed
region = ap-east-1

[profile staging]
source_profile = something
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/whatever/staging/authentication
# merely specify the default region;  not the only region used in this account
region = eu-west-1

[profile production]
source_profile = something
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::456789012345:role/whatever/production/authentication
# merely specify the default region;  not the only region used in this account
region = us-west-1
# ...
```
... and with a corresponding ~/.aws/credentials file containing:
```
# ...
[something]
aws_access_key_id = ASDFGHJKQWERTYUI1234
aws_secret_access_key = werpg98y3q4p0r9ur984h/45easdfseru23WFWERW4
# ...
```

Users would then need to create, multiple profiles to access resources stored in the same AWS account but different regions (or use native "awscli" log commands instead).

Perhaps it would be possible to add a "--region" option to complement the existing "--profile" option.

issue_title: Adding a callback to watch a prefix
issue_body:
Hi,

It looks like there is currently no way to add a callback to watch a prefix. Client's `add_watch_callback` method works perfectly when watching a key, but the only way to watch a prefix is to do it in a blocking way as shown in the README:

```python
watch_count = 0
events_iterator, cancel = etcd.watch_prefix("/doot/watch/prefix/")
for event in events_iterator:
    print(event)
    watch_count += 1
    if watch_count > 10:
        cancel()
```
When doing this in a dedicated thread, the execution is blocked as long as the `events_iterator` generator does not yield any value, and I'm not able to interrupt the thread. Am I missing something,  or should there be an `add_watch_prefix_callback` dedicated method?

## 主要更改

else:
    print('False')

print(r['BaseResponse'])
# {'Ret': 0}
```

## 完善多实例及退出

将增加新实例接口与退出接口

```python
itchat.new_instance()
itchat.logout()
```

## 更新构架

将本处于__init__.py中的方法也加入client

将client以初始化时加载形式组织

定义时方法仅作占位及注释作用

class Core(object):

        ''' place for docs
         * will be initialized in messages
        '''
        raise NotImplementedError()
```

```python

def load_components(core):

So I've been thinking about this.  We support syntax to covert case of groups etc. in replace templates, but currently, we just lower case or upper case according to whether the string is a byte string or Unicode string.

Should we reference the search pattern and restrict upper case and lower case to ASCII range if the search pattern had the `re.ASCII` flag set?  Not sure what a user should expect from this feature.  I think I'm a bit too close.   We only support Unicode and the `C` locale, but theoretically, if we did properly support locale, it might make sense to support upper casing and lower casing by locale.  With that logic, if the `ASCII` or `LOCALE` flag is set, maybe we should restrict our casing to ASCII...

I need to think about this.

make_class is a way to dynamically create classes, similar in spirit to collections.namedtuple or attr.make_class.

The question is, what should the API be for specifying fields?

For 'normal' usage, a field requires 3 pieces of information, one of which is optional: name, type, default value. The default value can be overridden with a field() call to specify not only the default value, but additional per-field attributes (use in hash, use in rear, etc.).

For make_class, I propose the per-field API be similar: 2 required items (name, type) and one optional one, the default or field().

So, something like:

    C = make_class('C',
                   [('a', int),
                    ('x', float, 1001.0),
                    ('b', str, field(default=1000, repr=False),
                   ],
                   bases=(B1, B2))

Which would be the dynamic equivalent of the static:

    @dataclass
    class C(B1, B2):
        a: int
        x: float = 1001.0
        b: str = field(default=1000, repr=False)

I realize the dynamic make_class call is somewhat unsatisfying, but I don't expect it to get used at all with statically specified values. My use case is something like reading database schemas and generating a dataclass that will be the return value for each row. In that case, the list of fields would be generated in code after reading the schema.

Hi! Thank you for checking out this issue!

Currently, this whole part is pretty much __open__. As of now, I'm planning to give PySwarms four major optimization capabilities: 

* single-objective continuous optimization
* single-objective discrete optimization
* multi-objective optimization, and 
* constrained optimization. 

We've established some grounds on single-objective continuous optimization (with the standard implementations of global-best and local-best PSO). But we haven't done anything yet as for discrete optimization. __Would you like to give us a headstart?__

These are the steps that will be undertaken to close this issue:
1. __Creating an abstract class in the `pyswarms.base` module.__ This will provide a skeleton on how other implementations of the same optimization nature would be written. Take for example how global-best (`pyswarms.single.gbest`) and local-best (`pyswarms.single.lbest`) are inheriting from the class `SwarmBase` (this is the abstract class for single-objective continuous). 

2. __Implementing a standard PSO algorithm inheriting the abstract class written in Step 1.__ This means that a particular discrete PSO optimization algorithm will be implemented while inheriting the base class. 

3. __Writing unit tests and use-case examples__ This is to show how the proposed skeleton and algorithm will be used by the end-user, and of course some unit tests to check its robustness (please check the `tests` directory)

As you can see, the steps is asking for a lot of things. Right now, we're setting this a __low-priority__ because I am currently writing the abstract classes for the other PSO variants. If you think you wanted to be a super-contributor, then go ahead and do all the steps above. 👍 😄 But I believe it would be much better if I set-up a basis first then we iterate from there.

__But perhaps, the best way to contribute on this issue would be the following__:
(note that these contributions don't require pull requests)
* __Propose features on how to implement the abstract classes.__ What do you think are the things to consider when making an abstract class for discrete PSO? You can use your domain-knowledge, and your past experience in handling discrete optimization problems to point out some helpful guides on how to set-up the abstract classes. I can take all of these into consideration when making the first commit in this issue.
* __Suggest discrete PSO implementations that can be implemented in the future.__ If you're planning to do this, please link the paper where it came from (it's okay if there's paywall). It would be better if the research is highly-cited, and is coming from reputable journals in the field of computational intelligence.

That's it for this issue!! For any questions, just drop a comment here!

Hi,

This is a question rather than issue.

I would like to access Friend's email address via Facebook. Assuming that, my application is still in beta I would like to find out:

a. Whether, if someone approves access to (email, user_profile and user_friends) by accessing application - does it cover user's email scope?

b. Does python-social-auth allow retrieval of Friend's email address? If yes, what scope needs to be used?

c. If above requires extended permission, does it require application review process?

With best,
Rajan

The [`Association` model](https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/df6d841bbcad0a800cc0cf1c4395e0cb2531aabb/social/apps/django_app/default/models.py#L88) inherits from DjangoAssociationMixin, which [performs a lookup when logging in](https://github.com/omab/python-social-auth/blob/master/social/storage/django_orm.py#L125-L126). This lookup relies on the `server_url` and `handle` columns.

Due to the lack of indices on these columns this lookup results in a full table scan, which is non-performant for applications with large user bases.

**Recommendations**

1. Add a compound index on the `server_url` and `handle` columns of the `Association` model.
2. Update the documentation recommending that those classes inheriting `DjangoAssociationMixin` include the same compound index.

## CME Version (cme --version)
Kali release (3.1.5 Smidge)
Bleeding Edge (4.0.1dev - Bug Pr0n)

## OS
Kali 2017.3

## Target OS
Windows 7 x64

## Detailed issue explanation
As per https://github.com/byt3bl33d3r/CrackMapExec/wiki/Command-Execution, cme should default to using WMI first for executing commands as per the failover methods.
In a lab environment, TCP/445 (SMB) was intentionally blocked between the Kali VM running cme and Windows 7 64bit target to ensure WMI would be used.

While monitoring network activity when executing commands however, it was observed that WMI is never attempted by cme and that it actually tries SMB first, even when explicitly specifying to use the wmiexec method via the command line args.

It was expected to see connectivity over TCP/135 (for WMI) however cme never tries, and due to the intentional firewall blocking TCP/445 during these tests, the cme commands failed.

Please see the attached screenshots.

It's strange but there are different code realization for `conditional_probability_alive` for `ModifiedBetaGeoFitter` and `BetaGeoFitter` with the same link on [Computing P(alive) Using the BG/NBD Model](http://www.brucehardie.com/notes/021/palive_for_BGNBD.pdf):
```
class BetaGeoFitter(BaseFitter):

    def conditional_probability_alive(self, frequency, recency, T):
        """
        Compute the probability that a customer with history (frequency, recency, T) is currently
        alive. From http://www.brucehardie.com/notes/021/palive_for_BGNBD.pdf

        Parameters:
            frequency: a scalar: historical frequency of customer.
            recency: a scalar: historical recency of customer.
            T: a scalar: age of the customer.

        Returns: a scalar

        """
        r, alpha, a, b = self._unload_params('r', 'alpha', 'a', 'b')
        return 1. / (1 + (frequency > 0) * (a / (b + frequency - 1)) * ((alpha + T) / (alpha + recency)) ** (r + frequency))

class ModifiedBetaGeoFitter(BetaGeoFitter):

    def conditional_probability_alive(self, frequency, recency, T):
        """
        Compute the probability that a customer with history (frequency, recency, T) is currently
        alive. From http://www.brucehardie.com/notes/021/palive_for_BGNBD.pdf

        Parameters:
            frequency: a scalar: historical frequency of customer.
            recency: a scalar: historical recency of customer.
            T: a scalar: age of the customer.

        Returns: a scalar

        """
        r, alpha, a, b = self._unload_params('r', 'alpha', 'a', 'b')
        return 1. / (1 + (a / (b + frequency)) * ((alpha + T) / (alpha + recency)) ** (r + frequency))
```

[conditional_probability_alive BetaGeoFitter](https://github.com/CamDavidsonPilon/lifetimes/blob/master/lifetimes/estimation.py#L622)

[conditional_probability_alive ModifiedBetaGeoFitter](https://github.com/CamDavidsonPilon/lifetimes/blob/master/lifetimes/estimation.py#L777)

issue_title: request.patch got an unexpected keyword argument 'json'
issue_body:
I'm trying to update the subscription status of a user with help of your nice little library and got into an issue:

```python
MailChimp('username', 'key')
	.member
	.update('listID', 'userHash', {'status': 'unsubscribed'})
```

which results in following traceback:
```
File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/mailchimp3/entities/member.py", line 29, in update
    return self._mc_client._patch(url=self._build_path(list_id, 'members', member_id), data=data)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/mailchimp3/mailchimpclient.py", line 25, in wrapper
    return fn(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/mailchimp3/mailchimpclient.py", line 96, in _patch
    r = requests.patch(url, auth=self.auth, json=data)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/requests/api.py", line 110, in patch
    return request('patch', url,  data=data, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/requests/api.py", line 44, in request
    return session.request(method=method, url=url, **kwargs)
TypeError: request() got an unexpected keyword argument 'json'
```
Looking into Pythons requests `patch` method shows:

```
def patch(url, data=None, **kwargs):
    """Sends a PATCH request.

    :param url: URL for the new :class:`Request` object.
    :param data: (optional) Dictionary, bytes, or file-like object to send in the body of the :class:`Request`.
    :param \*\*kwargs: Optional arguments that ``request`` takes.
    :return: :class:`Response <Response>` object
    :rtype: requests.Response
    """

    return request('patch', url,  data=data, **kwargs)
```

I think when you change `requests.patch(url, auth=self.auth, json=data)` to `requests.patch(url, auth=self.auth, data=data)` everything should work.

Maybe I'm wrong and dont see my fault, but this seems obvious.

Hi,

I am facing a random error in my tests, with django 1.8.12, coverage 4.0.3 and django_coverage_plugin 1.3 (and seemingly only on my CI server using ubuntu precise 32bit, not our developer machines using ubuntu >= 14.04 64bit):
```
django_coverage_plugin/plugin.py", line 170, in line_number_range
    assert frame.f_code.co_name == 'render'
AssertionError
```

(Side note, as it is an AssertionError exception, the test reports it as a failure, not an error)

It seems to be called from ``django/template/engine.py``, line 168 (https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.8.12/django/template/engine.py#L168):
```python
    def get_template(self, template_name, dirs=_dirs_undefined):
        """
        Returns a compiled Template object for the given template name,
        handling template inheritance recursively.
        """
        # ... removed for brevity
        if not hasattr(template, 'render'):  # <<-- fails here
            # template needs to be compiled
            template = Template(template, origin, template_name, engine=self)
```

This call comes from a fairly standard ``return render_to_string(template_name, context.flatten())`` in a template tag.

I've managed to get a shell at the point of failure, and ``frame.f_code.co_name`` is indeed ``'get_template'``.

Found this https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/blob/master/src/cloudant/database.py#L450

looks like we reference `db_updates` rather than `changes` in the example code.  Should be `changes`.

Cloudant versions based on CouchDB 2.0 store view dbcopy information in the design document epi options config:
```javascript
{"options": {"epi": {"dbcopy": {"myview": "mycopy"}}}}
```
whereas historically it was stored in the view object:
```javascript
 {"views": {"myview": {"map": "...", "reduce": "...", "dbcopy": "mycopy"}}}
```

This means that it cannot always be retrieved from the same dict location as previously. See additional discussion:

https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/pull/206#discussion_r74218882
https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/pull/206#discussion_r74221218
https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/pull/206#discussion_r75280808
https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/pull/206#discussion_r75288949

- Cloudant (python-cloudant) version(s) that are affected by this issue.
    - 2.x
- Python version 
    - Any
- A small code sample that demonstrates the issue.
    - Trying to retrieve the value of `dbcopy` from the old location fails e.g. when running `tests.unit.design_document_tests.DesignDocumentTests.test_fetch_map_reduce` against a new version

issue_title: How to include Negative samples/images for training
issue_body:
How to add negative samples while training. If i have my own datasets for 2 classes (e.g laptop and tvmonitor) and i have to include negative images/samples (which are not laptop and tvmonitor) then how should i add those images in configuration or in labels config.

Reason being to put this forward is after trained fully my network recognises window screen (with cover and some dim sunlight) as tvmonitor so i need to feed some negative samples to overcome this issue.

Or kindly suggest me some alternative to overcome this issue.
Hi, sorry... but I trained coco using this mxnet SSD, and i found that the mAP ( iou:[0.5]) is only about 0.15. But caffe+ssd can achieve 0.46 (iou:[0.5]).   Thanks.

```{r mxnet iterator}

G_iterator<- function(batch_size){

    batch<- 0
    batch_per_epoch<-5

    reset<- function(){
        batch<<- 0
    }

    iter.next<- function(){
        batch<<- batch+1
        if (batch>batch_per_epoch) {
            return(FALSE)
        } else {
            return(TRUE)
        }
    }

    value<- function(){
        set.seed(123+batch)
        digit<- mx.nd.array(sample(0:9, size = batch_size, replace = T))
        data<- mx.nd.one.hot(indices = digit, depth = 10)
        data<- mx.nd.reshape(data = data, shape = c(1,1,-1, batch_size))
        return(list(data=data, digit=digit))
    }

    return(list(reset=reset, iter.next=iter.next, value=value, batch_size=batch_size, batch=batch))
}

D_iterator<- function(batch_size){

    batch<- 0
    batch_per_epoch<-5

    reset<- function(){
        batch<<- 0
    }

    iter.next<- function(){
        batch<<- batch+1
        if (batch>batch_per_epoch) {
            return(FALSE)
        } else {
            return(TRUE)
        }
    }

    value<- function(){
        set.seed(123+batch)
        idx<- sample(length(train_label), size = batch_size, replace = T)
        data<- train_data[,,,idx, drop=F]
        label<- mx.nd.array(train_label[idx])
        digit<- mx.nd.one.hot(indices = label, depth = 10)

        return(list(data=mx.nd.array(data), digit=digit, label=label))
    }

    return(list(reset=reset, iter.next=iter.next, value=value, batch_size=batch_size, batch=batch))
}

```{r Generator Symbol}
data <-  mx.symbol.Variable('data')
gen_rand <- mx.symbol.normal(loc=0, scale=1, shape=c(1, 1, random_dim, batch_size), name="gen_rand")

G_sym <- mx.symbol.concat(data = list(data, gen_rand), num.args = 2, name="gen_concat") %>%
    mx.symbol.Deconvolution( name='g1', kernel=c(4,4), num_filter=gen_features*4, no_bias=T) %>%
    mx.symbol.BatchNorm( name='gbn1', fix_gamma=fix_gamma, eps=eps) %>%
    mx.symbol.Activation( name='gact1', act_type='relu') %>%
    mx.symbol.Deconvolution( name='g2', kernel=c(3,3), stride=c(2,2), pad=c(1,1), num_filter=gen_features*2, no_bias=no_bias) %>%
    mx.symbol.BatchNorm(name='gbn2', fix_gamma=fix_gamma, eps=eps) %>%
    mx.symbol.Activation(name='gact2', act_type='relu') %>%
    mx.symbol.Deconvolution(name='g3', kernel=c(4,4), stride=c(2,2),
                            pad=c(1,1), num_filter=gen_features, no_bias=no_bias) %>%
    mx.symbol.BatchNorm( name='gbn3', fix_gamma=fix_gamma, eps=eps) %>%
    mx.symbol.Activation( name='gact3', act_type='relu') %>%
    mx.symbol.Deconvolution( name='g4', kernel=c(4,4), stride=c(2,2), pad=c(1,1), num_filter=image_depth, no_bias=no_bias) %>%
    mx.symbol.Activation( name='G_sym', act_type='tanh')

label = mx.symbol.Variable('label')

> INFO:kazoo.client:Closing connection to SNIP:2181
> INFO:kazoo.client:Zookeeper session lost, state: CLOSED

What could be the reason? Am I missing a flag which is not documented maybe?

Thanks!
Jarko

More of a question than an issue. I'm writing a node library on top of the fb-watchman node binding and I have question about cleanup:

* Do I need to `watch-del` the directory I watched? Or is it enough to just unsubscribe?
* What if the user has other programs using that watched directory, will I be messing that up?
* If I don't `watch-del`, is that a leak?

Thanks

From the [current HEAD of `python/pywatchman/__init__.py`](https://github.com/facebook/watchman/blob/master/python/pywatchman/__init__.py#L546-L559):

```python
    def capabilityCheck(self, optional=None, required=None):
        """ Perform a server capability check """
        res = self.query('version', {
            'optional': optional or [],
            'required': required or []})

        if not self._hasprop(res, 'capabilities'):
            # Server doesn't support capabilities, so we need to
            # synthesize the results based on the version
            capabilities.synthesize(res, opts)
            if 'error' in res:
                raise CommandError(res['error'])

        return res
```

The variable `opts` is undefined anywhere; it isn't a local, a global or imported. Judging by the JavaScript version of the method from the [initial commit that added this code](https://github.com/facebook/watchman/commit/0fdbb1ede899e3f3b85f57141e1262618e583bd8), it should be `optional` instead of `opts`.

issue_title: contrib.kubernetes: Incompatibility with cluster auto-scaling and race conditions
issue_body:
Issue #2334 introduced a use case where running a Job on a cluster with limited resources leads to "stuck" tasks, waiting for the needed cluster resources to appear. The PR #2335 then changed the behavior to fail immediately when containers are not all in ContainerCreating/Running state after creating the job.

This approach has the following problems:
- It is fundamentally incompatible with auto-scaling clusters. In such clusters, Unschedulable pods are normal - they are a signal to the cluster to spin up a few more VMs, which will handle the increased load (after they spend some time booting, of course).
- Many events in Kubernetes happen in an async way. For example, there is no guarantees that the job pods are already created after the job itself is finished creating, so expecting the pods to exist can fail due to a race condition.

I propose that due to async nature of Kubernetes the fast-failing should be an exception, not the norm. For example, #2334 could be solved in this way: if the user-specified setting says that the cluster is not auto-scaling AND there are any pods with condition["PodScheduled"].reason == "Unschedulable", fail fast.
And in other cases the luigi task should just patiently wait until the objects reach the desired state.

(There is a proposed progressDeadlineSeconds extension for jobs that can accurately detect jobs stuck in initialization, unfortunately, it is not yet implemented - kubernetes/kubernetes#58384)

@yonromai, what do you think?

The mesh blades are miss aligned from the body mesh.

$ assimp export dragon.obj dragon.gltf
assimp export: select file format: 'gltf' (GL Transmission Format)
Launching asset import ...           OK
Validating postprocessing flags ...  OK

$ ls -l
total 87760

        "version": 1,
        "generator": "Open Asset Import Library (assimp v3.2.122064037)"
    },
    "scene": "defaultScene"

``

Environment: Windows 8, Python 3.5.2 Anaconda 64 bit

It says [here](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/tree/master/port/PyAssimp) that:

> PyAssimp requires a assimp dynamic library (DLL on windows, .so on linux, .dynlib on macOS) in order to work. The default search directories are:
> - the current directory

However, it does not seem to search the current path for me and prints this instead:

> raise AssimpError("assimp library not found")
> pyassimp.errors.AssimpError: assimp library not found

To fix this, I added the following line [here](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/port/PyAssimp/pyassimp/helper.py#L54) but I am not sure if this is the nicest way to do it so no pull request from me

> additional_dirs.append('.')

After that, I checked the error which was thrown (and silenced) [here](https://github.com/assimp/assimp/blob/master/port/PyAssimp/pyassimp/helper.py#L235):
> [WinError 193] %1 is not a valid Win32 application

Apparently this error is thrown when I try to load a 32 bit assimp.dll with 64 bit python and I should have used the 64 bit one instead.

Now all works well.

issue_title: Fix ignored and select settings interface with pycodestyle
issue_body: On https://github.com/PyCQA/pycodestyle/pull/722 they refused to fix their interface. When passing the list arguments as `ignore` and `select` settings to `pycodestyle`, it is required to pass a python list as `["E201", "E501"]`, instead of a string `"E201,E501"`, otherwise they will cause the issue pointed on: https://github.com/tomv564/LSP/issues/244#issuecomment-358753274

62

54

issue_title: Update DLLs for Python 3.7 on Windows
issue_body:
Just wanted to bring your attention to https://github.com/python/cpython/pull/5119 and [bpo-32507](https://bugs.python.org/issue32507), which will add many more DLLs in some cases that will need to be copied to virtual environments. You can see the files [here](https://github.com/python/cpython-bin-deps/tree/redist-1/x64/ucrt) (for 64-bit, but they are identical filenames for 32-bit). These will be installed alongside `python37.dll` on machines that do not already have a proper install of the C Runtime.

I had a quick look through the sources here and it appears that they won't automatically be copied. It also wasn't immediately obvious whether the OpenSSL DLLs will be copied (they are not statically linked any more). Those are in the `DLLs` directory and named `libssl-1_1.dll` and `libcrypto-1_1.dll` or `libssl-1_1-x64.dll` and `libcrypto-1_1-x64.dll`. There may already be bug reports as the early 3.7 releases include these.

It should be possible to run upgrades using the command line.
``ftw.upgrade`` should also be a zc.recipe or provide a script entry point.
The script should use the JSON API (see #4).

It is a design decision that it should not be a bin/instance run like script but depend on a running Plone site. This makes everything faster and better scriptable since it allows to query first for plone sites and then run upgrades for example.

Deciding which ZEO client (port) should be connected to could be done by either environment variables or with buildout options when a special recipe is provided. Maybe it also possible to guess available clients from the buildout and try-and-error for a connection.

issue_title: Returning ambiguous naive datetimes when tz info is available

Originally this was a bit more confusing because dateparser returned both a naive datetime object while also converting it using the local timezone offset. issue_title: elliptical_overlap_grid generating nan's in some cases
issue_body:
When setting up regression tests for ``elliptical_overlap_grid``, I ran into a number of failures where the masks were filled with ``nan``. The ones I tracked down all had equal semi-major and semi-minor axis sizes.  An example failure case is:

```
In [4]:
m = elliptical_overlap_grid(-1, 1, -1, 1, 1000, 1000, .8, .8, 0.5, 1, 10)
m.mean()
Out[4]:
nan
```

Slightly changing one of the axis values yields appropriate numbers:

```
In [13]:
m = elliptical_overlap_grid(-1, 1, -1, 1, 1000, 1000, .8, .80001, 0.5, 1, 10)
m.max()
Out[13]:
1.0000000000001465
```

Setting ``use_exact`` to 0 also yields correct results in all cases so the bug is somewhere within the ``use_exact`` code.

We get these requests from time to time, so I wonder whether we should at least make a zenodo entry for photutils (unless there are some other ideas in the pipeline).

cc @larrybradley @eteq

issue_title: Imports are done incorrectly in Stripe Python library
issue_body:
The Python API package does imports incorrectly. This is what you do:

`from stripe.resource import ...
`

This is what you should do ...

`from resource import ...
`

Why does it matter? Right now, stripe must be at the root of the web server. There is no reason for this and many web sites have packages such as Stripe in a subdirectory. Changing the imports would enable this without any disadvantages.

Support noted that there is an expectation that Stripe is installed globally. That is impossible on Google App Engine and is also not a best practice for people who only have one web server. It makes the server code more fragile because installs must happen before it is moved. Aside from that, fixing this problem would not cause any problems for globally-installed copies of Stripe.

Interestingly, there are comments in your __init__.py which indicate that your developers even know about this bug and haven't fixed it yet.

I noticed that `FluentSender` has `**kwargs`, but it's a blackhole—The values are never used.

Is there a reason for that to be there?  If not, it should be removed.

This would be a backwards-incompatible change, so it would need to wait until the next major release.

issue_title: Add "numerical" function (opposite of C() in formulas)?
issue_body:
It would be nice to have a function, say "N( )", that indicates that input data with pandas dtype "category" should be interpreted as numerical data. In that sense this function would do the exact opposite of C().

Why on earth would I want to do this? Say I have a column 'HoD' (Hour of Day) in the input dataframe whose datatype is a pandas "category" (the data I am working with is huge, so I want to save RAM and not have millions of int64 that take only 24 values). For a simple linear model I just run a vanilla formula specification without using C() and dummy coding is done automatically, that's great. But I also want to run some non-parametric estimators, and for those I want to avoid dummy-coding data. Having such a function would allow to use the same analysis pipeline without having to convert the input data in some cases.

issue_title: Members list of cluster not refreshed after reconnect
issue_body:
Given the following scenario: 
- A cluster made up of a single Java Hazelcast member.
- A single Hazelcast Python client that connects to the cluster through this Java member.

If the Hazelcast member gets disconnected or crashes, the Python client attempts to reconnect a few times (refer to the function ClusterService._reconnect at cluster.py) and if the Hazelcast member goes back online soon enough, the Python client nicely reconnects. 

The problem is that quite possibly, the Hazelcast member ID changes after being restarted and for some reason this change does not get reflected in the list of members kept by the Python client (specifically,  ClusterService.members). Thus, if we get a message say, on a queue, and we attempt to get the member that delivered the message through:

hz_python_client.cluster.get_member_by_uuid(cur_msg.issuingMemberId)

We will get None, even though cur_msg.issuingMemberId is part of the cluster.

I'm not really sure why, but the issue gets fixed by clearing the list of members before attempting to reconnect. We did so by modifying the function ClusterService._reconnect like this:

    def _reconnect(self):
        try:
            self.logger.warn("Connection closed to owner node. Trying to reconnect.")
            del self.members[:]
            self._connect_to_cluster()

This slight modification causes the event ClusterService_handle_member to be triggered immediately after reconnecting, which in turn properly updates the members list. Certainly, this looks more as a hack than as a nice solution. Could you please check it out?

Thank you!

Some of the image setup logic for chromatic drawImage fails when trying to set the output image properties of the convolution of two inseparable profiles with the nx=, ny=, scale= keywords.  For example:

```
import galsim
import numpy as np
waves = np.arange(500, 1000)
psf = galsim.ChromaticAiry(lam=700, diam=0.1)
bp = galsim.Bandpass(lambda x: 1, blue_limit=500, red_limit=1000)
sed1 = galsim.SED(galsim.LookupTable(waves, waves**0, interpolant='linear'),
                  flux_type='fphotons').withFlux(1.0, bp)
sed2 = galsim.SED(galsim.LookupTable(waves, waves**1, interpolant='linear'),
                  flux_type='fphotons').withFlux(1.0, bp)
g1 = galsim.Sersic(n=1, half_light_radius=1.0) * sed1
g2 = galsim.Sersic(n=2, half_light_radius=0.5) * sed2

img = galsim.Convolve(g1+g2, psf).drawImage(bp) # works
img = galsim.Convolve(g1, psf).drawImage(bp, nx=32, ny=32, scale=0.2) # works
img = galsim.Convolve(g1+g2, psf).drawImage(bp, nx=32, ny=32, scale=0.2) # fails
```

issue_title: adjust header file paths to ease building against GalSim library
issue_body:
I'd like to build against the GalSim compiled library, in order to create a measurement plugin in the LSST stack that delegates to the GalSim HSM module.

Unfortunately, the headers in GalSim are setup in such a way that it seems that multiple directories have to be added to the include path:
 - the `GalSim.h` include file is just in `include/`, and refers to all other headers as `galsim/${FILENAME}.h`.  That means we need `INCLUDE_PATH=${ROOT}/include/`
 - all the headers in the `include/galsim` directory refer to other files in that directory as just `${FILENAME}.h`.  That means we need `INCLUDE_PATH=${ROOT}/include/galsim`.
 - all the headers in directories below `include/galsim` (e.g. `include/galsim/hsm`) refer to all other headers relative to that directory, so we also need to put each of those directories in `INCLUDE_PATH`.

I think it'd make more sense to have all headers referred to the way they are in `GalSim.h` - so external libraries would only need to have `INCLUDE_PATH=${ROOT}/include`.

If there's agreement on that idea, I can have a PR ready by the end of the day (I'd like to move relatively quickly on this, as it's blocking some LSST pipeline work we'd like to do).

issue_title: Allow short flags, similar to Ansible
issue_body:
Our command line flags are too long and clunky. From the Ansible help menu, here are some examples of how Ansible core flags:

>   -f FORKS, --forks=FORKS
>   -e EXTRA_VARS, --extra-vars=EXTRA_VARS
>   -s, --sudo            run operations with sudo (nopasswd) (deprecated, use

tower-cli has these options or a simple option, but none of them have the short form. It has the `--forks` flag, but won't accept a `-f` flag.

For an example of allowing a shorthand flag, see `utils/decorators.py`. This shouldn't be hard to do, but we should go through all of the files in `resources/`, and use shorthand flags wherever they don't conflict with existing flags and would be helpful.

issue_title: Can not print a resource's fields
issue_body:
This is relevant for anyone using tower-cli as a python library.

Create some resource, like `res = tower_cli.get_resource('foobar')`, then try to inspect `res.fields`. This should be a list, but an error is encountered half way through:

```
[<Field: name (str, unique)>, <Field: description (str, not required)>, *** AttributeError: 'function' object has no attribute 'replace'
```

This appears to be due to the fact that [our custom types](https://github.com/ansible/tower-cli/blob/master/lib/tower_cli/utils/types.py) don't have [a `__name__` attribute](https://github.com/ansible/tower-cli/blob/master/lib/tower_cli/models/fields.py#L71). We should to go through all our custom types and assure that `__name__` is there and that the `__repr__` function works.

It looks like our inherited `ParamType`s from `click` also don't have this stuff, so there may be some potential for contributing upstream.

Thanks

With the `pykerberos` fork effectively going dark (see discussion at https://github.com/02strich/pykerberos/pull/27) and Apple's original ccs-pykerberos being apparently erstwhile maintained, I'm wondering what we can do to be on a supported Kerberos layer that's being actively maintained? pywinrm has a need to merge some specific IOV wrapping code that can only be implemented with low-level access to the C library and structures- we've got the code written, but Apple appears to have gone dark again (even if I could get the changes merged, I have no confidence that we could get any future bugfixes merged/released quickly). One option might be https://github.com/pythongssapi/python-gssapi - it uses Cython to provide access to both a high-level API and the underlying C API, so we could actually reimplement what we have in pure Python if this library were used. Biggest problem I can see there would be the Windows stuff that's mirroring the pykerberos library- we'd have to effectively split the implementation or write an interposer that would mimic the pykerberos API (since AFAIK there's no Windows equivalent available). 

We might (at least temporarily) take the gssapi approach with pywinrm and vendor a hacked requests-kerberos inside it, as that frees us from the reliance on third parties to get bugs fixed and released, but I'm curious if the requests team has any thoughts or different ideas about this problem in general?

issue_title: Draw distinction between bytes in blob_value (datastore property type) and large objects in GCS
issue_body:
Per discussion today, we should rollback #263 and document that users should use cloud storage for blobs instead. Quickly scanning the code, another option would be to leave #263 in place but to document that it shouldn't be used for large byte strings and to potentially raise an error over a certain size. ISTM the code would fail if we tried to insert a blob that is large enough (10MB?).

True blob support is really an artifact of the old datastore implementation (just like `GQL`) which predates Cloud Storage.

issue_title: BigQuery: request object not populated correctly for array query parameters
issue_body:
I recently documented how to use query parameters in BigQuery
https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/querying-data#bigquery-query-params-arrays-api

I found a problem with how the query parameters are supported in the client library. It does not set the type to `ARRAY`.

Also `arrayType` and `arrayValues` need to be in objects themselves. This is because arrays can also contain STRUCTs (but not other ARRAYs). Handing structs in arrays should maybe be a separate issue, though.

I was able to fix this for scalar types in https://github.com/tswast/google-cloud-python/commit/909cf52f26937a0e9758e87f2635cbc8de52e4bd but tests need to be updated, plus we should have integration tests.

issue_title: Filter `No processor claimed any of these annotations: ` from zinc output when compiling Java
issue_body:
In the twitter Compiler wrapper used with Jmake, there is an option to filter warnings using a regular expression.  This is used to suppress one error from javac in particular:

```
[warn] warning: No processor claimed any of these annotations: ...
```

In our environment, these warnings come out on almost every invocation of javac, which is a lot now that we've turned on the 'isolated' compile strategy.
The `invoke_runnable` extern is shaped like python `__call__`, and could replace a few other externs (`store_snapshot`, etc) with some minor tweaks.

It would be nice to have, for example:
```
./pants go-env golang/src/hopper:: -- subl
```
This would launch Sublime with a proper GOROOT and GOPATH in its environment and allow plugins like [GoSublime](https://github.com/DisposaBoy/GoSublime) to just work (checked that this in fact is the case).  This would be a quick way to expose the correct pants go workspace under .pants.d/ to tooling.

As due diligence when supporting this, test out with the [GoOracle](https://github.com/waigani/GoOracle) sublime plugin and [vim-go](https://github.com/fatih/vim-go) since these are known-used 3rdparty tools that @kellyellis and @vinibaggio could benefit from support for.

issue_title: Add some randomness when compiling intermediate deps when there are multiple candidates to build
issue_body:
We have a CI builder that launches builds in parallel.  Some of these CI workers are sharded builds of the same set of tests.  When there  are lots of stale dependencies to build, they all execute the build in the same order, and encounter the same stale deps at the same time.  When pants encounters this dependency relationship:

a -> B 
a -> C
a -> D

It would save overall build time if some of the shards build B, others built C first, and others built D.  Right now, everyone pounces on 'B' and then everyone pounces on 'C', then everyone tries 'D'.

Thanks for the tool. Its pretty awesome.

Is there a way to create multiple testsuite with this package ?

Thanks

issue_title: distributed_wait function in hahelpers/cluster.py isn't optimal for non-sequential unit numbers
issue_body:
distributed_wait function in contrib/hahelpers/cluster.py relies on function modulo_distribution in core/host.py.

The modulo_distribution function assumes the unit numbers will be close together, say units 0,1,2.
However, if you add and then remove units, you may have say 3 nodes of units 0, 3, 6 which all give the same wait time when using a modulo of 3.
For tasks like service restarts this can cause some patterns of unit numbers to all restart at the same time which isn't the desired outcome!

A potential alternative solution would be for each charm to use a peer relation (existing or new) to work out its sequenced wait time based on the number of units.

issue_body:
ITSM that ExaBGP currently needs to have the peer-as of a neighbor defined. For certain situations it can be useful to be able to accept any peer-as. Technically this would work by looking in the BGP OPEN message, read the AS of the peer and use that as the peer-as.

IIRC the end that first sends OPEN is not linked to the end that initiated the TCP connection. Rather the OPEN message is based on DelayOpenTimer, so ExaBGP can initiate the TCP connection but wait for the remote neighbor to send its OPEN message first. This way we don't have to passively wait for an incoming connection. Although not crucial to this, I would appreciate if ExaBGP could be configured rather aggressively regarding initial connections (I'm running it in a CI environment and waiting a minute for BGP means all the testing takes much longer).

Having nnpy on PyPi will benefit everyone who build package around it.
I think an sdist could be uploaded without any trouble.

issue_title: what should we do: empty GPOS/GSUB tables handling differences between Glyphs.app and fontmake
issue_body:
Now that we are working on single weight existing script conversions that include OpenType tables, we’ve encountered a new situation where Glyphs app. and pipeline handle things differently.
 
It has to do with empty GPOS/GSUB tables.
 
When we developed the SOW 12 TTF fonts, it was decided that if a font had a functional GSUB table, but no requirement for a GPOS, then an empty GPOS would be included. The reverse was also true about empty GSUBs.
 
In such a TTF, if there was an empty GPOS table it was truly empty, in that there were no features/lookups mentioned, just the script of the font.
 
It seems that a truly empty GPOS table won't work for OTFs produced via pipeline. When generating an OTF from a Glyphs file (with either FontWorker style OpenType or internal OpenType) via Pipeline, an empty GPOS table produces an error message in the log and no OTF is made. It appears the empty GPOS needs to have a feature and a lookup for pipeline to work.
 
A similar situation exists for an empty GSUB table. Pipeline requires there to be some feature and lookup present in the table.
 
Our question is how you want to handle fonts that have an empty GPOS or GSUB table? Is this something that can be fixed in pipeline? Or, are there some ‘dummy’ entries you want us to put in empty tables as a work around to this pipeline expectation?

issue_title: Updated story to reference the original story in JSON output
issue_body:
# What problem are we trying to solve?

Currently, there is no reference/link between an updated story that has been published and the original story. This is the case for the JSON output sent by Superdesk via the HTTP Push mechanism.

The problem with this is that client applications relying on the JSON output have no idea that an updated story is actually an update of a previously published story, they receive the story and see it just as any other new story.

Related user story: https://dev.sourcefabric.org/browse/SDESK-539

# How to test this

Pre-requisite: Make sure your Superdesk instance is configured to output a JSON version of the story on publishing.

1. Log into Superdesk, create a new story and publish it
2. Check out the JSON output of the published story. It should look something similar to [this](https://gist.github.com/hlmnrmr/a6d123e9df33e7973c9c3f6927ccfeb2)
3. Update the story and publish it
4. You will see now the JSON output sent to the client looks similar to [this](https://gist.github.com/hlmnrmr/8228e955b0c7e37cb96c72208fed8273).

# Solution

The solution is obviously to include the reference to the original story as part of the JSON output of the updated story, as you can see in [this Gist](https://gist.github.com/hlmnrmr/78d7a2164bc86cadd728dca2bbfb5d81)

With that, client applications can handle the reference in whichever way they prefer, for example:
- Overwriting the original story
- Publishing the update (and any subsequent updates) and next to it the original story

issue_title: Diamond leaving around zombie children of collector processes
issue_body:
I see child processes of diamond collectors occasionally become and stay zombies.  The processes that have become zombies are (ping and unbound-control).  I am also seeing messages in the log "Took too long to run! Killed!" in the logs.

From looking at the code (I am running something very close to master as of May 16th with a few small modifications to some of the collectors not related to this problem).  These external programs are invoked by the UnboundCollector and PingCollector which both use the run_command() found in collector.py.  run_command() uses subprocess.Popen(command, stdout=subprocess.PIPE).communicate().  The collect loop is run via utils/scheduler.py:collector_process().  This also controls the timeout behavior with signal alarm and the timeout SIGALRMException prints the log message "Took too long to run! Killed!".

I believe what is happening is that the signal.alarm is triggered because these processes (ping and unbound-control) are sometimes taking longer than the allowed timeout to run.  This alarm interrupts the wait() call inside of subprocess.communicate().  The python collector thread carries on to try again at some future time.  The ping/unbound-control commands eventually die, but since the wait() call was interrupted their parent (the collector thread) never comes back to check their exit code and those processes are left around as Zombies.

Has anyone else seen this issue?

Does anyone have a proposed fix?

Thanks!

Apologies if this is a configuration option I have missed, but I am having an issue with the HTTPJSONCollector talking to a Hystrix metrics endpoint in a Java application. It collects everything just fine, but inserts an extra dot in the collected data, regardless of whether we set the `path`, or how long this path is in terms of elements.

No other configuration other than `enabled` and `url`, which yields entries like this;

```
servers.net.example.appserv.httpjson..hystrix.ThreadPool.x.x 0 1471943878
```

The Hystrix endpoint is the default configuration, as far as I know. Is this expected behaviour, an error in the data, or some mismatch between collector and endpoint?

JSON data is formatted like this;
```
{"hystrix.Command.x.x":0,"hystrix.Command.x.x":0}
```
Much more extensive than that, but the above is the basic format; all on one line, key-value pairs separated by commas, curly braces, validates as proper JSON.

Thanks!

issue_title: Easy way to view musicXML in a browser
issue_body:
I ran into this excellent library for rendering sheet music in-browser using MusicXML, and wondered if it could be used with music21: [Open Sheet Music Display ](https://github.com/opensheetmusicdisplay) Turns out it is possible with a bit of js-hackery! I've posted the script below. This makes it easy to display scores without installing any other programs (apart from a web browser). The script requires the following file in the same directory (I can't upload .js files here, but it works fine as .txt). It's simply a compiled version of the OSMD project. [opensheetmusicdisplay.min.js.txt](https://github.com/cuthbertLab/music21/files/2058083/opensheetmusicdisplay.min.js.txt) This should display a random score from the corpus, enjoy! ```python3 from music21 import * import webbrowser, os, random, pathlib osmd_js_file = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)),"opensheetmusicdisplay.min.js.txt") if not pathlib.Path(osmd_js_file).is_file(): print("ERROR: need ./opensheetmusicdisplay.min.js.txt at",osmd_js_file) exit() print("found required .js") selected_piece = random.choice(corpus.getPaths()) print('loading piece', selected_piece) b = corpus.parse(selected_piece) def stream_to_web(b): html_template = """ Music21 Fragment may take a while to load large XML... 

			
		

	"""

	osmd_js_path = pathlib.Path(osmd_js_file).as_uri()

	filename = b.write('musicxml')
	print("musicXML filename:",filename)
	if filename is not None:
		with open(filename,'r') as f:
			xmldata = f.read()
		with open(filename+'.html','w') as f_html:
			html = html_template.format(
				data=xmldata.replace('`','\\`'),
				osmd_js_path=osmd_js_path)
			f_html.write(html)

		webbrowser.open('file://' + os.path.realpath(filename+'.html'))


stream_to_web(b)
```

issue_title: Ensure errors received from device prevent creation of LB components

issue_title: create_bucket should use secure transport if external s3 is used and it should respect s3 region
issue_body:
As discussed in #3947.

1. `s3secure=false` is completely [ignored](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/registry/templates/create_bucket#L12). In case of local s3 transport it may be fine, but for amazon I would like to use https if possible.
1. boto's [create_bucket](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/registry/templates/create_bucket#L17) with single name param will always create bucket in US region, it may cause higher bill if you have bucket in different region than deis and for sure it will degrade performance. It's fine if bucket is created before setup.
1. boto's [get_all_buckets](https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/registry/templates/create_bucket#L16) method requires special s3 permission, which I don't wan't to give deis s3 user. Maybe we can use `lookup`?. I've prepared [IAM policy](https://gist.github.com/szymonpk/07f3a0f18d78e6821983) which may be included into doc. It will limit permissions to just necessary ones, however I don't like `ListAllMyBuckets` which is forced by `get_all_buckets`.

issue_title: conda create -n creates an empty environment even if it fails
issue_body: This is because the history file gets written with the command. I think the history file should not be written until the end. @ilanschnell

issue_title: conda create --clone creates broken env when crossing filesystems
issue_body:
The conda create subcommand attempts to create hard links from source (non-symlink) files in the cloned env to the destination env.  However, when source and destination directories are on different filesystems, most commonly in an NFS environment, creation of those hard links fails.  The resulting environment has many broken links to shared libraries, as the actual shared library contents never get copied.

Creation of the hard links is attempted in conda.install._link when link type is LINK_HARD.  The underlying call to os.link throws an exception, but the exception is caught by the install.link() function and logged somewhere.  No error message gets written to the console.

The use of the --copy option in conda create does not change the behavior: hard links are still attempted, but silently fail.

I suggest either (1) detecting cross-filesysystem clones and copy files in that case instead of trying to hard link them; or (2) make --copy change the behavior of conda.install._link to use LINK_COPY.

In addition, the catching of the OSError exception in conda.install.link seems misguided.  Failure to create a link means the created env is in all likelihood broken.  The install should just quit and report the error on the console, and ensure that the script exits nonzero.

issue_title: in conda 4.4.0, conda upgrade -all removes all packages
issue_body:
After a new install of anaconda, do 

conda remove anaconda
conda upgrade --all

conda 4.4.0 will ask to remove all installed packages.

After revert to conda 4.3.31, conda upgrade --all acts correctly 
(except of course wishing to upgrade conda to 4.4.0)

System is Mac OSX Sierra on 2017 iMac.

If you search for "permission" in the issue tracker you'll find lots of people who have suffered greatly from Conda starting to do something and then borking out because of a permission problem:

https://github.com/conda/conda/issues?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=is%3Aissue+is%3Aopen+permission

There are too many to list here, and I don't think it would help for me to include the most recent time (just now) when this has happened to me -- because it is one of the variants many other people have already reported.

I would suggest that Conda should check permissions **first**, then warn or abort if there are issues.  A frequent outcome of this problem is an inoperative Anaconda instance, requiring full reinstallation  (there are a few manual recovery strategies, but they require some expert insight into how things work).

Additionally if a permissions problem (or `OSError`) does crop up it should be caught and a helpful error message passed to the user.  Instead right now we return a stack trace.  Yuck.

issue_title: Support for CSI indexed BAM files
issue_body:
Pysam currently does not support random access for BAM files indexed using the 'CSI' format (e.g. via samtools index -c input.bam), which is required for alignments involving sequences longer than the maximum of 2^29 - 1 bp supported by the BAI index format.

However, support for CSI is already part of HTSLib, and as far as I can tell, the only thing preventing the use of CSI index files (for local files) in Pysam is the following check in libcalignmentfile.pyx:

                    if self.is_bam \
                            and not os.path.exists(filename + b".bai") \
                            and not os.path.exists(filename[:-4] + b".bai"):
                        self.index = NULL
                        has_index = False

https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/56cc5ba6b5c91bf9127056ca8f5d2e6e0c583d76/pysam/libcalignmentfile.pyx#L627-L631

Simply changing the above to something like the following appears to be sufficient for random access to 'CSI' indexed BAM files, based on my limited testing:

                    if self.is_bam \
                            and not os.path.exists(filename + b".bai") \
                            and not os.path.exists(filename[:-4] + b".bai") \
                            and not os.path.exists(filename + b".csi") \
                            and not os.path.exists(filename[:-4] + b".csi"):
                        self.index = NULL
                        has_index = False

But as I am not comfortable enough with the Pysam code-base to be sure that this is all that needs to be changed, and I am opening this issue as a feature-request, rather than doing a pull request.

Cheers

issue_title: Bad wording in `--help` for blob operations
issue_body:
(Ubuntu, azure-cli 2.0.27, and showing storage as 2.0.25.)

The `--help` shown for many `storage blob` commands show:

    --if-modified-since   : Alter only if modified since supplied UTC datetime (Y-m-d'T'H:M'Z').
    --if-unmodified-since : Alter only if unmodified since supplied UTC datetime (Y-m-d'T'H:M'Z').

The `Alter` there is wrong, since some of these commands do not actually
alter anything.  Would be better to use "operate only on blobs if ..."
or something similar.

issue_title: [Compute] Availability Set create defaults can be invalid
issue_body:
The following defaults:
```
--platform-fault-domain-count : Default: 5.
--platform-update-domain-count: Default: 5.
```
can be invalid depending on the region the availability set is created in. For example, when I tried to create a VM in westus with an availability set in westus, I got a deployment error requesting I use a value between 1 and 3.

We should either set the default to something that works across all regions or see if there's a way to query what the default should be for the selected region.

issue_title: DOC: document that Signal handlers are run sequentally
issue_body:
http://aiohttp.readthedocs.io/en/stable/web.html#signals

This link does not mention significant thing: signal handlers are run sequentally in the order they was registered, even if their handlers are asynchronous.

While running a server with aiohttp 3.0.1 using Python 3.6 on Windows 7, I'm occasionally encountering this error:
```
Task exception was never retrieved
future: <Task finished coro=<RequestHandler.start() done, defined at C:\Program Files\Python36\lib\site-packages\aiohttp\web_protocol.py:340> exception=IndexError('pop from an empty deque',)>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Program Files\Python36\lib\site-packages\aiohttp\web_protocol.py", line 365, in start
    message, payload = self._messages.popleft()
IndexError: pop from an empty deque
```
I'm not sure what requests are causing this, but it seems to be because it's possible for the Cython version of the HTTPRequestParser to return an empty tuple for `messages` through HTTPParser's `feed_data`.

issue_title: Batchrunner report creation error when values are one of dict/list/ndarray
issue_body:
in batchrunner.py:
`ordered[param] = self.fixed_parameters[param]`

When values are a dict/list/np.ndarray, the above line throws the error:
ValueError: Length of values does not match length of index

This is because pandas interprets these datatypes as multiple values to fill a column with.

I used the following solution:
```
val = self.fixed_parameters[param]
if (type(val) == dict) or (type(val) == np.ndarray) or (type(val) == list):
    ordered[param] = str(val)
else:
    ordered[param] = val
```

It does the job for me, but it doesn't account for potential other datatypes that may have the same problem.  So not a very clean solution.  Any ideas how to handle this better?

`GetBuiltProjectOutputGroup` task (from c:\Program Files (x86)\MSBuild\Microsoft\VisualStudio\v14.0\DotNet\Microsoft.DotNet.Tasks.dll assembly) ignores `outputName` defined in `project.json`. This means that, any build system that uses `BuiltProjectOutputGroup` target to collect output of the project, will not be able to find result assembly.
**tested on:**
.NET Core SDK for Windows-rc2
**example:** 
msbuild ProjectName.xproj /t:BuiltProjectOutputGroup /v:diag
**project.json:**
```
{
  ...
  "buildOptions": {
    "outputName": "OutputName"
  }
}
```
**result:**
```
  Output Item(s):
      _BuiltProjectOutputGroupOutput=
          C:\...\ProjectName\bin\Debug\netstandard1.6\ProjectName.dll
```
**expected:**
```
  Output Item(s):
      _BuiltProjectOutputGroupOutput=
          C:\...\ProjectName\bin\Debug\netstandard1.6\OutputName.dll
```

The [pathlib](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pathlib.html) module was added to the standard library in Python 3.4. The expected behavior is to be able to path either a string or a `pathlib.Path` object when referencing a file or directory. For example, this behavior is demonstrated in `open`, `gzip.open`, and `os.walk`.

**Example expected behavior in mutagen:**
```
import pathlib
from mutagen.id3 import ID3

path = pathlib.Path('somefile.mp3')
audio = ID3(path)
```

issue_title: Strange bitrates reported, e.g. 320141 instead of 320000
issue_body:

>>> f = mutagen.File('Tours_-_01_-_Enthusiast.mp3')

Audio Bitrate                   : 320 kbps
