issue_title: Unexpected huge performance difference between solver using "default" tactic, Z3_mk_solver() and Z3_mk_simple_solver()
issue_body:
I'm currently investigating queries that Z3 is taking a very long time to solve in my tool and I ran into one that Z3 is handling in a surprising way.

This is the query

```
(declare-fun f_ackermann!0 () (_ BitVec 64))
(assert
 (not (fp.isNaN ((_ to_fp 11 53) f_ackermann!0))))
(assert
 (let ((?x8 ((_ to_fp 11 53) f_ackermann!0)))
(let ((?x18 (fp.sqrt roundNearestTiesToEven (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x8 ?x8))))
(let ((?x24 (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x18 ((_ to_fp 11 53) (_ bv4607092346807469998 64)))))
(let ((?x11 (fp.abs ?x8)))
(let (($x20 (or (fp.isNaN (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x11 ((_ to_fp 11 53) (_ bv4607092346807469998 64)))) (fp.isNaN ?x18))))
(let (($x22 (or $x20 (fp.gt (fp.mul roundNearestTiesToEven ?x11 ((_ to_fp 11 53) (_ bv4607092346807469998 64))) ?x18))))
(not (or $x22 (or (or (fp.isNaN ?x11) (fp.isNaN ?x24)) (fp.lt ?x11 ?x24)))))))))))
(check-sat)
```

In my tool libz3 is taking ~1679 seconds to solve this. When I give this query to the Z3 binary it is solving the query in ~2 seconds.

I started digging into this and by making my tool increase libz3's verbosity it looks like the slow `smt` tactic is being used when my tool uses libz3.

```
(smt.searching)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 4)
(smt.searching)
(smt.simplifying-clause-set :num-deleted-clauses 30)
...
```

but when Z3 binary solves the query a different tactic is being used.

```
(sat.sat-solver)
(sat.simplify)
(sat-restart :conflicts 202 :decisions 677 :restarts 1 :clauses 185638 :learned 159 :gc-clause 0 :memory 46.75 :time 1.35)
(sat-restart :conflicts 303 :decisions 1100 :restarts 2 :clauses 185638 :learned 246 :gc-clause 0 :memory 46.75 :time 1.36)
...
```

In my tool I use the `Z3_mk_simple_solver()` to create the solver which I expected (maybe this is my mistake?) to have the same performance as the Z3 binary if no explicit tactic is requested and no logic is set. However it seems this is not the case at all.

To investigate some more I tried creating the Z3 solver instance in two different ways in my tool

* replacing `Z3_mk_simple_solver()` with `Z3_mk_solver()`
* Explicitly create the `default` tactic and make a solver from that, i.e. like this
```c++
    theTactic = Z3_mk_tactic(builder->ctx, "default");
    Z3_tactic_inc_ref(builder->ctx, theTactic);
    theSolver = Z3_mk_solver_from_tactic(builder->ctx, theTactic);
```

In both cases these changes to my tool caused performance to improve dramatically on these queries. Looking at the verbose output it seems when I instantiate Z3's solver this way it looks like the much faster `sat.sat-solver` tactic gets used.

So why is this happening? I would have expected `Z3_mk_simple_solver()` to behave like the Z3 binary does when no logic is provided and no tactic is supplied.

To help you reproduce this I'm attaching [z3_interaction.zip](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1018519/z3_interaction.zip) (Z3 interaction log) and 
[solver-queries.core_solver.aa.smt2.txt](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1018523/solver-queries.core_solver.aa.smt2.txt) (also a log of queries issued to Z3 in SMT-LIBv2.5 format).

Warning the interaction log has
* Multiple contexts. One is used for solving, the other is just used for logging queries to a text file.
* Sets verbosity to 0

**CC:** @ccadar @danielschemmel

issue_title: Will Z3 ever "collapse" model entries in v2 format?
issue_body:
Consider the following simple input file:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(declare-fun myfun ( Int ) Int)
(assert (! (= (myfun 1) 1)))
(assert (! (= (myfun 2) 2)))
(assert (! (= (myfun 3) 1)))
(check-sat)
(get-model)
```
When I run this through Z3 using the "model.v2=true" option, I get the following output:
```
sat
"myfun -> {
  1 -> 1
  2 -> 2
  3 -> 1
  else -> 1
}"
```
In this case, Z3 did not "collapse" the two entries that yield the value 1; every application of the function to a unique set of arguments is explicitly listed.  Is this guaranteed to always be the case, or can there be cases where collapsing can occur?

Thanks!

Hello,


I am working on a symbolic executor to analyze some program, and I am writing to ask for help on speeding up the constraint solving of `Z3 Java`. 

What I am basically trying to do is to iterate the whole program, symbolize some variables, and collect certain path conditions along the code. When encountering certain criteria, I will pause my symbolic executor and put all the collected path conditions (they are `BoolExpr`) into the solver and let it check whether there exist a satisfiable solution regarding the symbolized variables. (of course it is indeed more complex but I try to simplify the presentation..)

 I give one path condition example below, in which `symbol1` is the symbolized variable. As you can see, the formula itself is not that large, but according to my observation, when I have have around 10~20 such kind of formulas, the solver takes several seconds to check the satisfiability.

    `(134821342,(let ((a!1 (ite (= ((_ extract 2 2) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000002
                (ite (= ((_ extract 1 1) symbol1) #b1) #x00000001 #x00000000))))
    (let ((a!2 (ite (= ((_ extract 4 4) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000004
                (ite (= ((_ extract 3 3) symbol1) #b1) #x00000003 a!1))))
    (let ((a!3 (ite (= ((_ extract 6 6) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000006
                (ite (= ((_ extract 5 5) symbol1) #b1) #x00000005 a!2))))
    (let ((a!4 (ite (= ((_ extract 8 8) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000008
                (ite (= ((_ extract 7 7) symbol1) #b1) #x00000007 a!3))))
    (let ((a!5 (ite (= ((_ extract 10 10) symbol1) #b1)
                #x0000000a
                (ite (= ((_ extract 9 9) symbol1) #b1) #x00000009 a!4))))
    (let ((a!6 (ite (= ((_ extract 12 12) symbol1) #b1)
                #x0000000c
                (ite (= ((_ extract 11 11) symbol1) #b1) #x0000000b a!5))))
    (let ((a!7 (ite (= ((_ extract 14 14) symbol1) #b1)
                #x0000000e
                (ite (= ((_ extract 13 13) symbol1) #b1) #x0000000d a!6))))
    (let ((a!8 (ite (= ((_ extract 16 16) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000010
                (ite (= ((_ extract 15 15) symbol1) #b1) #x0000000f a!7))))
    (let ((a!9 (ite (= ((_ extract 18 18) symbol1) #b1)
                #x00000012
                (ite (= ((_ extract 17 17) symbol1) #b1) #x00000011 a!8))))
    (let ((a!10 (ite (= ((_ extract 20 20) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x00000014
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 19 19) symbol1) #b1) #x00000013 a!9))))
    (let ((a!11 (ite (= ((_ extract 22 22) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x00000016
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 21 21) symbol1) #b1) #x00000015 a!10))))
    (let ((a!12 (ite (= ((_ extract 24 24) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x00000018
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 23 23) symbol1) #b1) #x00000017 a!11))))
    (let ((a!13 (ite (= ((_ extract 26 26) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x0000001a
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 25 25) symbol1) #b1) #x00000019 a!12))))
    (let ((a!14 (ite (= ((_ extract 28 28) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x0000001c
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 27 27) symbol1) #b1) #x0000001b a!13))))
    (let ((a!15 (ite (= ((_ extract 30 30) symbol1) #b1)
                 #x0000001e
                 (ite (= ((_ extract 29 29) symbol1) #b1) #x0000001d a!14))))
    (let ((a!16 (bvxor #x0000001f
                   (ite (= ((_ extract 31 31) symbol1) #b1) #x0000001f a!15))))
    (let ((a!17 (bvshl symbol1
                   (bvand (concat #x000000 ((_ extract 7 0) a!16)) #x0000001f))))
    (let ((a!18 (ite (= (bvand (bvadd a!17 a!17) (bvadd a!17 a!17)) #x00000000)
                 #b1
                 #b0)))
      (= a!18 #b0))))))))))))))))))))
`


Of course seconds are acceptable, however, eventually the path conditions could grow quite large, and there exists hundreds or even thousands of them.. I do observed that the solver becomes much slower regarding those more challenging situations... 

So here is my question, given my situation (solving hundreds median/large size constrains with one or several symbols),  how should I start to speedup the solving process? I can come up with several options:

1. Cache the already solved constrains to save the re-solving time ==> I implemented such strategy in my tool

2. Substitute some large composite formulas with multiple symbols into a fresh new symbol ==> this can speedup the process in sacrifice of false positive.

3. some other things..?

I do not know much about theory proving and constraint solving, and I am sorry if these seems too naive.. I am writing to ask, could you please sled light on some general approaches to speedup the solving process in my case? Any suggestion is appreciated. Thank you!

issue_title: Updates to optimize module in ML interface
issue_body:
The signature of get_lower and get_upper can be improved.
It currently has signature:

  (** Retrieve lower bound in current model for handle *)
  val get_lower : handle -> int -> Expr.expr

  (** Retrieve upper bound in current model for handle *)
  val get_upper : handle -> int -> Expr.expr


And the implementation is:
  let get_lower (x:handle) (idx:int) = Z3native.optimize_get_lower (gc x.opt) x.opt idx
  let get_upper (x:handle) (idx:int) = Z3native.optimize_get_upper (gc x.opt) x.opt idx

The intent with the handle is to encode opaquely the index that gets passed.
The implementation should be along the lines of:

  (** Retrieve lower bound in current model for handle *)
  val get_lower : handle -> Expr.expr

  (** Retrieve upper bound in current model for handle *)
  val get_upper : handle -> Expr.expr

with:

  let get_lower (x:handle)  = Z3native.optimize_get_lower (gc x.opt) x.opt x.h
  let get_upper (x:handle)  = Z3native.optimize_get_upper (gc x.opt) x.opt x.h

A few newer APIs can also be added:

val  from_file : optimize -> string -> unit
val  from_string : optimize -> string -> unit
val get_assertions : optimize -> Expr.expr list
val get_objectives : optimize -> Expr.expr list

From `src/tactic/smtlogics/qfbv_tactic.cpp`, I suppose Z3 takes the eager bit-blasting approach to solving a QF_BV constraint except when it is in the "QFBV_EQ" fragment (which will be fed to the DPLL(T)-style `smt_tactic`). 

However, when solving QF_AUFBV constraints (src/tactic/smtlogics/qfaufbv_tactic.cpp), Z3 will always call the `smt_tactic` after some preprocessing phases.  It is possible to implement a bit-blasting based tactic for QF_AUFBF? 

My goal is to solve QF_AUFBV by eager bit-blasting.  The basic idea is to use  `mk_bvarray2uf_tactic` and `mk_ackermannize_bv_tactic` for eliminating array and uninterpreted function symbols. 

However, my implementation got an exception in later the SAT engine when solving some queries from KLEE. 
~~~~
static tactic * mk_eager_qfaufbv_preamble(ast_manager& m, params_ref const& p) {
    params_ref solve_eq_p;
    //.. omit some paramters 
    return
            and_then(
                    mk_simplify_tactic(m),
                    mk_propagate_values_tactic(m),
                    using_params(mk_solve_eqs_tactic(m), solve_eq_p),
                    mk_elim_uncnstr_tactic(m),
                    mk_bv_size_reduction_tactic(m),
                    using_params(mk_solve_eqs_tactic(m), solve_eq_p),  // pinpoint: this can handle some "fast sat" cases

                    using_params(mk_simplify_tactic(m), simp2_p),
                    //using_params(mk_simplify_tactic(m), hoist_p),
                    mk_bvarray2uf_tactic(m, p),        // reduce bv array to bv(with uninterpreted function)
                    mk_ackermannize_bv_tactic(m, p), // TODO: do ackermannization here or after max_bv_sharing?
                    mk_max_bv_sharing_tactic(m)
            );

issue_title: Since Wagtail 1.12 localized StreamFields wrongfully become required
issue_body:
Since Wagtail 1.12 StreamField's `blank` defaults to `False` and this has exposed an issue in `wagtail-modeltranslation` where localized StreamFields wrongfully become required. This happens because the localized StreamField is a shallow copy of the original StreamField and the `required` attribute lives in `localized_field.stream_block.meta.required`.

As per [django-modeltranslation docs](http://django-modeltranslation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/registration.html?highlight=blank#required-fields) all localized fields should default to optional.

issue_title: Should theming be overriding base.html the way it does?
issue_body:
I spent an hour today working out why several Django 1.8 changes weren't showing up.

I'd used 'theming' to fix some CSS and it overrides base.html. So we need to update the included base.html to match the Django version

This means maintaining a different base.html for each Django version and figure out how to load the correct one for a given install.

Considering the only reasons for overriding it are to add a single tag loader and a tag that loads some CSS it strikes me that this is too high a price to pay.

There's also the problem that only one app can override base.html - if another app that tries the same technique then it's down to the ordering of INSTALLED_APPS

This can be partially solved with documentation - tell people what to put in their base.html if they need to - but it gets messy.

I wonder if this could be improved in Django core. I'm still mulling it over but I thought I'd start a conversation.

One potential new capability of the FunctionFeaturizer (or derivative of it) would be a ``fit()`` type method that would try a bunch of functions for a single variable and relate it to an output variable. Then, pick the functional form that best correlates with the output as your descriptor.

e.g., I have a descriptor like "avg mass" and want to correlate this with the output "thermal conductivity".

The FunctionFeaturizer would try things like:
- square of avg mass
- exp^(avg_mass)
- exp^(-avg_mass)
- 1/(avg_mass)
- ln(avg_mass)

And in the end, instead of creating a dozen new features, will just retain the one functional form that best correlates with the output. Thus there will only be a single feature for avg_mass (not a dozen), but it might have a different functional form.

RPM repositories have a feature that allows the user to request a particular checksum type to be used during repository publish. Unfortunately, this feature has never worked consistently from release to release, which is why Pulp issue 1618 was filed:

https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1618

pulp-smash could perform the following tests:
1. Create a repository with the checksum type set to type A and a feed URL that uses checksum type B (for example, you could configure a Pulp repository to use sha1 but connect it to a feed that uses sha256). Sync and publish the repository. The resulting published primary.xml.gz file should contain a list of all the RPMs in the repository. Assert that all of these RPMs are listed with checksum type A and not checksum type B.
2. Create a repository with the checksum type set to type A, and another repository with a checksum type of B. Put units in repo A that have checksum type A. Copy some or all of those units to repo B and publish repo B. Ensure that the primary.xml.gz file contains checksum type B and not checksum type A.
3. Create a repository with the checksum type set to some value. Upload an RPM to that repository and publish. Ensure that the published data is correct.
4. Perform the above, and then create a second repository with a different checksum type. Upload that same RPM to the second repository and publish. Ensure that the second repository's published data uses the correct checksum type.

I'm having some problems with my PPO implementation, so I'm browsing your code. 
Thank you so much for contributing this open-source. It's lots of fun to dig through your implementation :)

But there are some points that confuse me. Basically, my feedback is similar to that on A2C https://github.com/openai/baselines/issues/413 : It would be great if your code had more comments, there are some design differences between the implementations and you make some changes to the paper that are not explained.

I'm reading the code on ppo and ppo2. Here are some of the things I noted:

 - Naming schemes are completely different. In ppo your target advantage is called  [atarg](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/a6b1bc70f156dc45c0da49be8e80941a88021700/baselines/ppo1/pposgd_simple.py#L96). But in ppo2, you call the same thing [ADV](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/a6b1bc70f156dc45c0da49be8e80941a88021700/baselines/ppo2/ppo2.py#L21). Things like this are everywhere. By the way, the naming is not even consistent in itself. You also use [atarg](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/a6b1bc70f156dc45c0da49be8e80941a88021700/baselines/ppo1/pposgd_simple.py#L170) to describe a value which is not the placeholder.

 - Lack of code reuse, different coding styles. For example, GAE is calculated with a similar algorithm. You even have a helper function [here](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/a6b1bc70f156dc45c0da49be8e80941a88021700/baselines/ppo1/pposgd_simple.py#L64). But in ppo2, you don't use the same function and the algorithm looks slightly different. Another example for coding style differences would be the policies. MlpPolicy for example looks completely different but is mostly the same.

 - Huge architectural differences. In ppo you produce training data with a generator. In ppo2 you use an implementation of the Runner class. 

 - Nontrivial changes to the paper. I read the openAI blogpost and the ppo paper. The key innovation is looking at a clipped ratio of action probabilities. ppo does that. ppo2 also applies this clipping to the value function. I would consider that a big difference between ppo and ppo2, which is not explained or motivated at all. I mean intuitively it makes sense. But ppo2 was described as a faster, gpu-based version of ppo, and the differences seem to be more than that.

 - Nontrivial changes to the paper, part 2. The code is sprinkled with small tricks. For example, you apply z normalization to the advantage function, [here](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/a6b1bc70f156dc45c0da49be8e80941a88021700/baselines/ppo2/ppo2.py#L54). This one could be my lack of understanding. But I've only seen something like this in dueling q-learning. You substract the mean of the advantage and add the value. That might work well here, too.  - Differences between the policies. In ppo, the MlpPolicy setup takes this [if](https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/a6b1bc70f156dc45c0da49be8e80941a88021700/baselines/ppo1/mlp_policy.py#L36) branch. That seems like an important modification and honestly, I don't understand why it is necessary. In ppo2, there is no such code.

requirements TBD

According to #2488, `otLinkOutOfBandTransmitRequest()` is designed to be in conjunction with other mac operation which includes normal transmission. However, `otPlatRadioTransmit()` is designed to accept buffer provided by `otPlatRadioGetTransmitBuffer()` only(see https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/radio.h#L422), which returns a single static buffer on most example platforms. If  `otLinkOutOfBandTransmitRequest()` respect the definition of `otPlatRadioTransmit()` and get the buffer from `otPlatRadioGetTransmitBuffer()`, it could destroy data of normal transmission, and If `otLinkOutOfBandTransmitRequest()` ignores the design and constructs its own buffer, it may not be properly processed(at least on platform posix and nrf82540).

Shall we adjust the design of `otPlatRadioTransmit()` or shall we forbid initiating requests to send normal Thread frame and out of bound frame simultaneously?

issue_title: cli: add an output FIFO for the serial port
issue_body: The CLI currently does not have an output FIFO, which can cause characters to be lost if subsequent output is generated before the serial driver can transmit the first request.

The OpenThread project currently uses [astyle](http://astyle.sourceforge.net/) to automatically format code and provide baseline enforcement of the OpenThread code style.  In general, I've found astyle to be fairly limited in its flexibility.

This is a proposal to switch to [uncrustify](https://github.com/uncrustify/uncrustify).  For better (or worse), uncrustify supports many more options than astyle.  In short, astyle allows automated formatting and enforcement of more code style aspects, including:

- Left-aligned variable declarations.
- Aligned assignment operators.
- Left-aligned end-of-line comments.
- ... and many more.

issue_title: LSTM not predicting time series correctly
issue_body:
So I am trying to predict the stock market, and before you say anything about the stock market being impossible to predict, I predicted Twitter's stock on Friday down to a cent. But I was looking to move my network over to Tflearn from Keras. Here is both Keras and Tflearn network code:

Keras

           `model = Sequential()
            model.add(LSTM(layers[0], input_dim=look_back, return_sequences = True))
            model.add(Dropout(0.2))

            model.add(LSTM(layers[1], return_sequences=True))
            model.add(Dropout(0.2))

            model.add(LSTM(layers[2], return_sequences=False))
            model.add(Dropout(0.2))

            model.add(Dense(output_dim=1))
            model.add(Activation("linear"))

            start = time.time()
            model.compile(loss="mse", optimizer="adam")
            end = time.time()
            print ("Build Time: ", end - start)`

Tflearn

           `start = time.time()
            model = input_data(shape=[None, trainX.shape[1]])
            model = tflearn.embedding(model, input_dim=trainX.shape[1], output_dim=look_back)

            model = lstm(model, layers[0], return_seq=True, activation="sigmoid")
            model = dropout(model, 0.2)

            model = lstm(model, layers[1], return_seq=True)
            model = dropout(model, 0.2)
            
            model = lstm(model, layers[2], return_seq=False)
            model = dropout(model, 0.2)

            model = fully_connected(model, 1)
            model = activation(model, activation="linear")

            model = regression(model, optimizer = "adam", learning_rate=0.01, loss='mean_square')
            model = tflearn.DNN(model, tensorboard_verbose=0)
            end = time.time()
            print ("Build Time: ", end - start)`

With Keras, I was able to get great results, it was just very slow. But tflearn seems to operate faster, but my predictions range between 150, and -25, when changing around different parameters. But I kept 100 neurons on layer 1, 200 on layer 2, 300 on layer 3, and changed around things like activation, optimizer, and learning rate. But I can't get results anything near as close to keras.

I am happy to provide more code if necessary, but the code is the same between libraries, and the only change is the different code needed for each library.  What should I do?

Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Would you like to delete the cert(s) you just revoked?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(Y)es (recommended)/(N)o: n

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Congratulations! You have successfully revoked the certificate that was located
at /etc/letsencrypt/live/cert.eval.ohie.org/fullchain.pem

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```
And then I attempted to renew the certificate, now that the existing one is revoked.
```
$ certbot renew
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Processing /etc/letsencrypt/renewal/cert.eval.ohie.org.conf
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Cert not yet due for renewal

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following certs are not due for renewal yet:
  /etc/letsencrypt/live/cert.eval.ohie.org/fullchain.pem (skipped)
No renewals were attempted.
No hooks were run.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
I would expect that certbot would renew a revoked certificate; especially so, considering [in the documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#revoking-certificates) it is stated that:

> If you don’t use delete to remove the certificate completely, it will be renewed automatically at the next renewal event.

How can I make `certbot renew` automatically renew the revoked certificate? Do I have to enforce that I force renew after revocation? i.e., `certbot renew --cert-name __name__ --force renewal` after a revocation?

issue_body:
This issue is for checking untyped definitions in the `certbot-dns-dnsmadeeasy` module.

To start, add the following to `mypy.ini` in the root of the repo:
```
[mypy-certbot_dns_dnsmadeeasy.*]
check_untyped_defs = True
```

Then run `tox -e mypy`. If passes, submit a PR. If not, fix errors and submit a PR when tests pass. You can read more about how all of this works below.

See explanation here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fp0tPP9bzYkUViTnOXAyoT_KfgTfs8HSpNMoU8Tm_SY/edit?usp=sharing

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the `--check-untyped-defs` option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation! 

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean `--check-untyped-defs` output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase. 

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!). 

Once `mypy --check-untyped-defs` passes on your codebase, you should lock down your progress by running the mypy checker in your CI environment.

Because mypy type annotations are optional, once you’ve done the setup above, you can start annotating your codebase at whatever pace you like. Over time, you’ll get the benefits of static types in the parts of the codebase that you’ve annotated, without needing to do anything special to the rest of the codebase (the wonders of gradual typing!). In the next section, I’ll discuss strategy for how to move the codebase towards being fully annotated.

issue_title: As a user, I want to query the datasets using AND and OR clauses
issue_body:
As a user, I want to query the datasets using `AND` and `OR` clauses.

Today, the search aggregate the parameters with an implicit `AND` (ex: tag=tag1&tag=tag2).

We need an `OR` in many cases (typically MipyGeo).

Proposal of syntax:
- character `|`: `tag=tag1|tag2`
- character `,`: `tag=tag1,tag2`

# Problem Definition
Deploy a test network

## Environment

- blockchain: geth X ropsten (maybe later rinkeby)
- deployment: AWS

## Scenario

- 100 raiden nodes
- 3 ERC20 tokens
- `connect` token networks over time
- initiate transfers to other nodes
- receive transfers from other nodes
- `leave` token networks
- test interwoven transfers (token/mediation/direct)
- apart from some specific transfers it should have some transfer "background noise"


## Existing Infrastructure

- ansible AWS deployment scripts
- (predictable) node address/account generation scripts
- contract deployment scripts
- token deployment & distribution scripts
- simplified deployment by using raiden.AppImage (instead of installing python+dependencies)


## Missing/incomplete infrastructure

- public testnet connection with minimal sync overhead (trusted node(s))
- public testnet ETH faucet/distribution for participant addresses/accounts
- global scenario view
- scenario playbook control via raiden restful API/RPC (this should replace `scenario_runner.py` which was a modded raiden app instance)
- scenario assertions via raiden restful API/RPC ("node37 sent 5 and received 7 token Y, assert balance before settle")
- scenario assertions via blockchain (assert token balance after settlement: "node37 balance of token Y has changed '-2'")
- node error alarms (raiden processes should be monitored for livelyhood)
- central log-file collection (logs are stored on AWS instances, but recovery may be tedious with current implementation)


## Nice to have's

- global scenario instrumentation (connection graph, transfer success/failure rate, path lengths)

issue_title: Number of tasks per submitted job
issue_body:
Suppose I have this situation:

```
[1]
parameter: seed = [x for x in range(1000)]
input: for_each(['seed'])
task:
R:
  set.seed(${_seed})
  ...
```

This will generate 1000 tasks, therefore 1000 HPC jobs. This sounds like a burden in many ways. I wonder if we can have some task option:

```
task: task_per_job = 20
```
and, 

```
task: total_jobs = 50
```

In the first case, SoS will put 20 tasks in one HPC job and submit 50 jobs. In the 2nd case, we tell SoS that we want to submit 50 jobs total and SoS will figure out how many tasks per job, in this case is 20. 

I under stand we do have the `max_job` option. But that still will result in submitting 1000 jobs in the end, though sequentially reather than at once. I think these two options would be great features to have.

issue_title: Tests for configuration of remote hosts
issue_body:
Not sure how much we can do to set up remote hosts but perhaps we can provide some features to test the availability and proper configuration of remote hosts. Some thing like

1. If the host can be sshed without password. We can even help answer yes here.
2. If sos is installed on remote host.
3. If `shared` settings is correct. For example, if two hosts are said to share a drive, try to write a file and see the file can be seen locally.
4. If `paths` are correct, that is to say if the specified path exists.
5. test file copy permission because I got 
```
rsync: chgrp "/home/bpeng1/SoS_Demo/examples/.annotated_DEG_list.csv.mM66YO" failed: Operation not permitted (1)

issue_title: Invalid file_target created from functions in pathlib.Path
issue_body:
```
>>> from sos.runtime import file_target
>>> a = file_target('test.txt')
>>> a._md5
>>> b = a.with_suffix('.sos')
>>> b
file_target('test.sos')
>>> b._md5
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'file_target' object has no attribute '_md5'
```
The reason for this is that `a.with_suffix('.sos')` is called from the base class, which creates a `pathlib.Path`, but not the `_md5` attribute from `file_target`. I do not quite understand why this happens but we will need to fix it.

issue_title: check_cpl_reel_coherence: Encryption is not required to be homogenous throughout composition
issue_body:
Please relax the check on encryption coherence across reels with regards to encryption.

The below references do not mention encryption and indeed, I have several sample compositions containing a mix of encrypted and unencrypted trackfiles that playout without problems.

- ST 429-2:2013 section 8.7:
_Essence tracks in a Composition shall have homogenous encoding parameter values throughout the Composition. Picture essence shall have constant **frame rate** and **pixel array size**. Sound essence shall have constant **sample rate**, **language**, **channel count**, and **channel assignment parameters**._

- mpeg_ii_pack_constraints_2v5.doc section 7.2:
_The **edit rates** of all Assets must be equal and constant for the duration of the Composition._

- mpeg_ii_pack_constraints_2v5.doc section 7.3
_The following picture-related parameters shall not change for the duration of the Composition Playlist:
 -- **Raster size**
 -- **Screen Aspect Ratio**
 -- **Frame Rate**_

- mpeg_ii_pack_constraints_2v5.doc section 7.4
_All sound track files referenced by a given composition must share identical:
 -- **baseband sample rate**
 -- **baseband sample bit depth**
 -- **language**
 -- **channel format** (presentation format)_

- mpeg_ii_pack_constraints_2v5.doc section 7.5
All subtitle tracks referenced by a given Composition Playlist must share identical **language**.

I would like to be able to integrate Django Helpdesk with an issue tracker in order to auto-generate incidents/tasks and correspond from within that issue tracker.  The best way to do this would be to add bi-directional integration hook support where you can define the issue tracker API endpoint Django Helpdesk hits (and how it should format the data), and an API endpoint on Django Helpdesk that can take data as provided by the issue tracker.

For example: I would like to integrate Phabricator Maniphest tasks into Django Helpdesk (similar to how Sentry does it [here](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry-phabricator/blob/master/sentry_phabricator/plugin.py)).  I'd like to take it further such that it not only generates Maniphest tasks, but also allows for bi-directional communication within that task (updates to the Django ticket are reflected in the associated Maniphest task and vice-versa).

To do this, I would tell Django Helpdesk to connect to a Phabricator [Conduit](https://secure.phabricator.com/book/phabricator/article/conduit/) endpoint to create Maniphest tasks, and also configure a Phabricator [Herald event](https://secure.phabricator.com/book/phabricator/article/webhooks/) to send Phabricator events to Django Helpdesk.  Phabricator uses `PHID` identifiers, so Django Helpdesk would need to store an integration's task/issue identifier in order to retain proper correlation.

So.. how could I begin?  I took a brief look at the Django Helpdesk code but didn't find anything that exists to support this, so some sort of integration framework needs defined first.  To support specific integrations, I suppose a "translation" config would be simplest (e.g. integration "Foo" sends JSON issues[0].user.email which is provided by Django.email, and Django will sent its Django.email as JSON payload issue.user.email to the "Foo" API endpoint of "/blah/api").

Thoughts or suggestions?

from pythainlp import word_tokenize

Currently, properties only uses builtin exceptions. `ValueError` when validation fails, `AttributeError` for setting invalid attribute, and `TypeError` when Property parameters are invalid. Custom exception types could improve error handling by separating properties errors from other unrelated Python errors.
